metadata.title: EMA Reflection Paper on Investigation and Assessment of Cardiovascular Safety of Anticancer Medicinal Products
metadata.document_status: DRAFT — adopted by CHMP for public consultation 19 January 2026; consultation closes 31 July 2026. Non-binding reflection paper.
metadata.notes: This is a DRAFT reflection paper (non-binding) developed jointly by EMA Cardiovascular Working Party (CVSWP) and Oncology Working Party (ONCWP). Rules extracted at MEDIUM/LOW severity since this is a non-binding reflection paper currently under consultation. It provides EMA scientific committee positions on CV safety assessment in oncology trials — a topic not covered by any existing ICH guideline.

rules.rule_text: A tailored, risk-based approach to CV safety assessment is recommended for anticancer medicinal products, characterized by two extremes of CV risk: minor risk for products belonging to well-established classes with no known CV safety concerns, and substantial risk for novel pharmacological classes or those with non-clinical evidence of potential CV toxicity.
rules.verbatim_quote: A tailored, risk-based approach is recommended, characterized by two extremes of CV risk. At the lower end, risk is considered minor for new anticancer medicinal products belonging to a well-established pharmacological class with no known CV safety concerns. At the higher end, risk is considered substantial when the investigational product represents a novel pharmacological class or mechanism of action and is supported by non-clinical evidence indicating potential CV toxicity, or by CV safety signals emerging during clinical development.
rules.exceptions: Products where the risk category could not be easily assigned (e.g., due to novelty of class or very limited early clinical exposure) should default to the more detailed assessment approach.

rules.rule_text: For anticancer products with substantial CV risk or where risk category cannot be easily assigned, a more detailed assessment of CV safety should be considered at the planning stage of the registration trial to better estimate overall clinical effect in the intended population.
rules.verbatim_quote: For those medicinal products with a substantial risk and for those products where the risk category could not be easily assigned (for example due to the novelty of the class, very limited early clinical exposure), a more detailed assessment of CV safety is warranted that should be considered at the planning stage of the registration trial, in order to better estimate the overall clinical effect of the medicinal product in the intended population.

rules.rule_text: For low-cardiac-risk anticancer products, safety monitoring supported by clinical and nonclinical evaluation of QT/QTc interval prolongation and pro-arrhythmic potential may suffice, unless CV safety signals emerge that require further characterization.
rules.verbatim_quote: For the low-cardiac risk category of anticancer products, safety monitoring during clinical experimentation supported by a clinical and nonclinical evaluation of the QT/QTc interval prolongation and pro-arrhythmic potential may suffice, unless CV safety signals do emerge that would require further characterization.
rules.exceptions: If CV safety signals emerge during development, further characterization beyond basic QT assessment is needed regardless of initial risk classification.

rules.rule_text: Inclusion and exclusion criteria for oncology trials should consider baseline CV risk factors, previous exposure to cardiotoxic therapies, and the presence of underlying CV disease and other comorbid conditions.
rules.verbatim_quote: Inclusion and exclusion criteria should consider baseline CV risk factors, previous exposure to cardiotoxic therapies, and the presence of underlying CV disease and other comorbid conditions.

rules.rule_text: Patients with pre-existing CV conditions should not be excluded from oncology trials unless appropriately justified based on identified safety concerns, in order to avoid potential underestimation of CV risks associated with the treatment.
rules.verbatim_quote: The inclusion criteria should ensure that patients with pre-existing CV conditions are not excluded unless appropriately justified based on identified safety concerns, in order to avoid a potential underestimation of the CV risks associated with the treatment.
rules.exceptions: Exclusion is justified when patients are at an excessively high risk of CV events in order to protect them from potential serious consequences.

rules.rule_text: The study design should include a comprehensive baseline assessment of CV risk factors, including clinical history, physical examination, laboratory tests and imaging studies, to identify patients at higher risk of developing CV toxicities.
rules.source_section: Section 4 - Baseline assessment of CV risk factors
rules.verbatim_quote: The study design should include a comprehensive baseline assessment of CV risk factors, including clinical history, physical examination, laboratory tests and imaging studies. This characterisation may be necessary to help to identify patients who are at a higher risk of developing CV toxicities and allow for appropriate monitoring and management during the trial.

rules.rule_text: Key risk factors such as hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidaemia, and previous CV disease should be documented and considered in the analysis of CV safety data.
rules.source_section: Section 4 - Baseline assessment of CV risk factors
rules.verbatim_quote: Key risk factors such as hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidaemia, and previous CV disease should be documented and considered in the analysis of CV safety data.

rules.rule_text: Genetic predispositions affecting individual responses to cancer therapies should be considered. Variants in genes such as RARG, SLC28A3, UGT1A6, NAT2, and CYP2D6 have been associated with heightened cardiotoxicity risk, particularly for trastuzumab and anthracyclines. Incorporating genetic screening into inclusion/exclusion criteria could help identify high-risk patients.
rules.source_section: Section 4 - Baseline assessment of CV risk factors
rules.verbatim_quote: Notably, variants in genes such as RARG, SLC28A3, UGT1A6, NAT2, and CYP2D6 have been associated with heightened risk of cardiotoxicity, particularly in the context of therapies involving trastuzumab and anthracyclines. Incorporating genetic screening into inclusion/exclusion criteria could help identify high-risk patients and tailor treatment plans accordingly.

rules.rule_text: The design and duration of oncology trials should be appropriate to capture both short-term and long-term CV toxicities depending on the target indication. Randomized controlled trials with adequate sample sizes and appropriate control groups are the optimal strategy for robust CV safety assessment.
rules.verbatim_quote: The design and duration of oncology trials should be appropriate to capture both short-term and long-term CV toxicities depending on the target indication. Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) with adequate sample sizes and appropriate control groups are the optimal strategy for robust CV safety assessment.
rules.exceptions: Single-arm trials may use extended follow-up periods and real-world data as external control arms if methodology is pre-defined and scientifically sound.

rules.rule_text: The duration of follow-up should be sufficient to capture late-onset CV events, which may occur years after the completion of cancer therapy. A risk-based approach should define specific CV monitoring and adequate risk mitigation measures applicable post-marketing.
rules.verbatim_quote: The duration of follow-up should be sufficient to capture late-onset CV events, which may occur years after the completion of cancer therapy. To this end, a risk-based approach should be adopted to define specific CV monitoring and adequate risk mitigations measures to be applicable post-marketing.

rules.rule_text: Prospective definition and standardized classification of CV endpoints are crucial for consistent and reliable assessment of CV safety in oncology trials. CV endpoints should be defined according to consensus definitions from cardiology and oncology societies, and explicitly mapped to CTCAE for summary purposes.
rules.source_section: Section 6 - Definition of CV Endpoints, Reporting and Analysis
rules.verbatim_quote: Prospective definition and standardized classification of CV endpoints are crucial for consistent and reliable assessment of CV safety in oncology trials... These endpoints should be defined according to consensus definitions, from cardiology and oncology societies, and explicitly mapped to Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events (CTCAE) for summary purposes.

rules.rule_text: Based on safety pharmacology and pharmaceutical class, predefined specific CV events should be included in the protocol as adverse events of special interest (AESI).
rules.verbatim_quote: According to the expected safety profile of the product, based on safety pharmacology and pharmaceutical class, predefined specific CV events should be included in the protocol as adverse events of special interest (AESI).

rules.rule_text: The prospective definition of CV endpoints, ideally tailored to the expected safety profile of each specific product, should include both clinical and subclinical events. Subclinical events (changes in cardiac biomarkers or imaging findings) can provide early and more sensitive indications of cardiotoxicity.
rules.verbatim_quote: Moreover, the prospective definition of CV endpoints, ideally tailored to the expected safety profile of each specific product, should include both clinical and subclinical events. Clinical events, such as myocardial infarction, heart failure, and arrhythmias, are typically easier to identify and classify. However, subclinical events, such as changes in cardiac biomarkers or imaging findings, can provide early and more sensitive indications of cardiotoxicity and could potentially help to prevent more serious clinical events.

rules.rule_text: All CV events should be reported as adverse events with detailed documentation of the event severity, timing, and management. Standardized reporting templates and electronic data capture systems should be used to enhance accuracy and completeness.
rules.verbatim_quote: All CV events should be reported as adverse events (AEs), with detailed documentation of the event severity, timing, and management. The use of standardized reporting templates and electronic data capture systems can enhance the accuracy and completeness of CV outcome reporting.

rules.rule_text: Reporting of CV outcomes should include both clinical and sub-clinical events. Clinical events should be reported with detailed information on timing, severity, and management. Subclinical events (changes in cardiac biomarkers or imaging findings) should also be reported for comprehensive CV safety profile assessment.
rules.verbatim_quote: Moreover, the reporting of CV outcomes should include both clinical and sub-clinical events. Clinical events, such as myocardial infarction, heart failure, and arrhythmias, should be reported with detailed information on the timing, severity, and management of the event. Subclinical events, such as changes in cardiac biomarkers or imaging findings, should also be reported to provide a comprehensive assessment of the CV safety profile of the anticancer medicinal product.

rules.rule_text: A pre-specified safety meta-analysis of CV endpoints should be considered for anticancer medicinal products with a substantial risk of CV adverse effects. This requires systematic assessment and consistency of CV endpoint definitions maintained across trials.
rules.verbatim_quote: For that purpose, a pre-specified safety meta-analysis of CV endpoints should be considered for anticancer medicinal products with a substantial risk of CV adverse effects. This implies that systematic assessment as well as consistency of definitions of CV endpoints would be sought/maintained across the trials in order to strengthen the quality of the data available for B/R assessments.
rules.exceptions: Applies specifically to products with substantial CV risk; low-risk products may not require meta-analysis.

rules.rule_text: Pre-specified analyses of CV outcomes considering both investigator-reported data and adjudicated events is recommended whenever feasible.
rules.verbatim_quote: Pre-specified analyses of CV outcomes, considering both investigator-reported data and adjudicated events is recommended whenever feasible.
rules.exceptions: Such analyses will often be exploratory and underpowered for less frequent serious CV events.

rules.rule_text: Continuous CV safety monitoring during registration trials is essential, including regular assessments of cardiac biomarkers, ECGs, and imaging studies. Monitoring protocols should be tailored to the specific anticancer treatment and patient population, with predefined thresholds for intervention and dose modification.
rules.verbatim_quote: Continuous CV safety monitoring during registration trials is essential to detect and manage CV toxicities promptly. This includes regular assessments of cardiac biomarkers, electrocardiograms (ECGs), and imaging studies. Monitoring protocols should be tailored to the specific anticancer treatment and patient population, with predefined thresholds for intervention and dose modification based on the severity of CV events.

rules.rule_text: To address potential differential follow-up between treatment arms in registration clinical trials, post-treatment monitoring after the end of treatment is recommended. Such monitoring should be standardized across study arms to ensure consistency in data collection and outcome assessment.
rules.verbatim_quote: To address potential differential follow-up between treatment arms in registration clinical trials, it is recommended to include post-treatment monitoring after the end of treatment. Such monitoring should be standardized across study arms to ensure consistency in data collection and outcome assessment.

rules.rule_text: Clear protocols for the management of CV toxicities during trials must be established, including guidelines for dose modification, treatment interruption, or discontinuation based on the severity of the CV event. These should also bear on efficacy analyses as intercurrent events, considering ICH E9(R1).
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition to regular monitoring, it is important to establish clear protocols for the management of CV toxicities during the trials. This includes guidelines for dose modification, treatment interruption, or discontinuation based on the severity of the CV event. These have also a bearing on the efficacy analyses as intercurrent events, considering E9(R1).

rules.rule_text: Management information for CV toxicities needs to be included in the product information, as it is essential for healthcare providers to make informed decisions about the use of anticancer treatments.
rules.verbatim_quote: This information needs to be further included in the product information, as it is essential for healthcare providers to make informed decisions about the use of anticancer treatments.

rules.rule_text: The management of CV toxicities in oncology patients requires a risk-based and individually tailored approach, including dose modification and supportive care based on severity and recurrence of CV events. The CTCAE grading system should guide management with specific recommendations for dose reduction, interruption, or discontinuation.
rules.verbatim_quote: The management of CV toxicities in oncology patients requires a risk-based and individually tailored approach, including dose modification and supportive care based on the severity and recurrence of CV events. The CTCAE grading system can guide the management of CV toxicities, with specific recommendations for dose reduction, interruption, or discontinuation of cancer therapy, and the use of cardioprotective agents.

rules.rule_text: The RMP should include identified and potential CV risks and the need for additional Risk Minimisation Measures (aRMM), as well as detailed guidelines for management of CV toxicities including recommendations for baseline assessments, regular monitoring, and intervention strategies.
rules.verbatim_quote: The RMP should include the identified and potential risks and the need for additional Risk Minimisation Measures (aRMM), as well as detailed guidelines for the management of CV toxicities, including recommendations for baseline assessments, regular monitoring, and intervention strategies.

rules.rule_text: Labelling of anticancer medicinal products should clearly outline the potential CV risks associated with the treatment and provide guidance on the management of these risks. Data to support this information needs to be available at the time of marketing authorisation.
rules.verbatim_quote: The labelling of anticancer medicinal products should clearly outline the potential CV risks associated with the treatment and provide guidance on the management of these risks. This information is essential for healthcare providers to make informed decisions about the use of anticancer treatments and to implement appropriate monitoring and management strategies to minimize the impact of CV toxicities on patient outcomes. Data to support this information needs to be available at the time of the marketing authorisation.
rules.exceptions: For cases where it is difficult to delineate the CV safety profile before authorization, uncertainties should be managed under the RMP.

rules.rule_text: Post-marketing surveillance and real-world data (RWD) should further inform the RMP and support the safe use of anticancer medicinal products in clinical practice.
rules.verbatim_quote: Post-marketing surveillance and RWD can further inform the RMP and support the safe use of anticancer medicinal products in clinical practice.
